name: atlasmind
description: Developer-centric multi-agent orchestrator for VS Code with model routing, long-term memory, and skills registry.

chatParticipants.name: atlas
chatParticipants.description: Multi-agent orchestrator with model routing & memory

commands.name: bootstrap
commands.description: Initialise a new project with SSOT memory structure

commands.name: import
commands.description: Import an existing project by scanning files and populating memory

commands.name: memory
commands.description: Query or manage the SSOT memory system

commands.name: cost
commands.description: Show cost summary for the current session

commands.name: project
commands.description: Decompose a goal into tests-first subtasks and execute autonomously

commands.name: voice
commands.description: Open the Voice Panel for text-to-speech and speech-to-text

commands.name: vision
commands.description: Pick workspace images and ask a multimodal question

commands.name: runs
commands.description: Open the project run center and inspect recent autonomous runs

commands.title: AtlasMind: Import VS Code MCP Servers

walkthroughs.title: Get Started with AtlasMind
walkthroughs.description: Set up your first AI-powered multi-agent workspace in five steps.

steps.title: Configure a model provider
steps.description:
AtlasMind routes tasks across multiple providers and keeps search, voice, image, and video vendors on dedicated specialist surfaces.
[Open Model Providers](command:atlasmind.openModelProviders)
[Open Model Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsModels)

steps.title: Bootstrap or import your project
steps.description:
Create the SSOT memory structure for a new project, or import context from an existing one.
[Bootstrap Project](command:atlasmind.bootstrapProject)
[Import Project](command:atlasmind.importProject)

steps.title: Shape Atlas's personality profile
steps.description:
Open the guided Personality Profile to tune Atlas's role, tone, memory posture, and live workflow defaults. You can skip most prompts and leave them on auto if the current behavior already fits.
[Open Personality Profile](command:atlasmind.openPersonalityProfile)
[Open Project Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsProject)

steps.title: Send your first message
steps.description:
Type `@atlas` in the AtlasMind chat panel followed by a question or task. AtlasMind selects the best agent and model automatically.
[Focus Chat View](command:atlasmind.openChatView)
[Open Chat Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsChat)

steps.title: Run an autonomous project
steps.description:
Use `/project <goal>` to decompose a task into parallel subtasks with preview, approval gating, cost tracking, and checkpointing. If you need to shape the idea first, jump into the ideation whiteboard before you launch the run.
[Open Chat](command:atlasmind.openChatPanel)
[Open Project Ideation](command:atlasmind.openProjectIdeation)
[Open Project Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsProject)

viewsWelcome.contents:
No MCP servers configured.
[Open MCP panel](command:atlasmind.openMcpServers)
[Open Safety Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsSafety)

No routed models configured yet.
[Open Model Providers](command:atlasmind.openModelProviders)
[Open Model Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsModels)
[Open Specialist Integrations](command:atlasmind.openSpecialistIntegrations)

No project runs recorded yet.
[Open Project Ideation](command:atlasmind.openProjectIdeation)
[Open Project Run Center](command:atlasmind.openProjectRunCenter)
[Open Project Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsProject)

No sessions yet.
[Focus Chat View](command:atlasmind.openChatView)
[Open Chat Settings](command:atlasmind.openSettingsChat)

atlasmind.budgetMode.markdownDescription:
Budget preference for model selection.

Use `cheap` to keep routing on the lowest-cost acceptable models, `balanced` for the default mix, `expensive` to unlock premium reasoning models, or `auto` to let AtlasMind infer the tier from the task.

Examples:
- Solo scratchpad or note-heavy work: `cheap`
- Day-to-day engineering work: `balanced`
- Architecture reviews, migrations, or ambiguous debugging: `expensive`
- Shared team workspace with mixed task sizes: `auto`

atlasmind.speedMode.markdownDescription:
Speed preference for model selection.

Use `fast` to bias toward quick-response models, `balanced` for a normal tradeoff, `considered` to allow slower reasoning-heavy models, or `auto` to let AtlasMind choose based on task complexity.

Examples:
- Tight feedback loops while editing: `fast`
- General coding and chat: `balanced`
- Root-cause analysis or long-form planning: `considered`
- Teams switching between support, coding, and review: `auto`

atlasmind.feedbackRoutingWeight.description: How strongly saved thumbs-up or thumbs-down feedback should influence future model routing. Set to 0 to disable the feedback bias.
atlasmind.feedbackRoutingWeight.markdownDescription:
How strongly saved thumbs-up or thumbs-down feedback should influence future model routing. AtlasMind still caps the effect so votes cannot override capability, budget, speed, or provider-health gates.

Examples:
- Disable feedback-weighted routing: `0`
- Default slight bias: `1`
- Stronger but still bounded nudge: `1.5`

Use this when you want feedback to matter more or less without deleting the recorded vote history.

atlasmind.specialistRoutingOverrides.description: Per-domain overrides for specialist routing automation.

atlasmind.localOpenAiEndpoints.description: Labeled local OpenAI-compatible endpoints AtlasMind should aggregate under the Local provider.
atlasmind.localOpenAiEndpoints.markdownDescription: Labeled local OpenAI-compatible endpoints AtlasMind should aggregate under the Local provider. Use this when you want AtlasMind to see more than one local engine at the same time, such as Ollama plus LM Studio. AtlasMind uses the label back in provider surfaces so operators can tell which endpoint owns which local model.

atlasmind.localOpenAiBaseUrl.description: Legacy single local OpenAI-compatible endpoint fallback.
atlasmind.localOpenAiBaseUrl.markdownDescription: Legacy single local OpenAI-compatible endpoint fallback. AtlasMind prefers `atlasmind.localOpenAiEndpoints` when that structured list is present. Keep this only for backward compatibility with older workspace settings or lightweight single-endpoint setups.

atlasmind.azureOpenAiEndpoint.description: Azure OpenAI resource endpoint used for deployment-based chat routing, for example https://your-resource.openai.azure.com.
atlasmind.azureOpenAiEndpoint.markdownDescription:
Azure OpenAI resource endpoint used for deployment-based chat routing. This should be the resource endpoint, not a deployment or API path.

Example:
- `https://your-resource.openai.azure.com`

In larger environments, keep this aligned with the approved regional Azure OpenAI resource for your team, then manage access with SecretStorage-backed API keys and deployment allow-lists.

atlasmind.azureOpenAiDeployments.description: Azure OpenAI deployment names that AtlasMind should surface as routed models.
atlasmind.azureOpenAiDeployments.markdownDescription:
Azure OpenAI deployment names that AtlasMind should surface as routed models. Use deployment names exactly as they exist in Azure.

Examples:
- Single deployment: `["gpt-4.1"]`
- Split by workload: `["gpt-4.1-mini", "gpt-4.1", "o4-mini"]`
- Enterprise setup with separate safety-reviewed deployments per capability: `["chat-standard", "reasoning-premium", "vision-approved"]`

Keep this list intentionally small so only approved deployments appear in routing.

atlasmind.bedrock.region.description: AWS region used for Amazon Bedrock model invocations, for example us-east-1.
atlasmind.bedrock.region.markdownDescription:
AWS region used for Amazon Bedrock model invocations. AtlasMind signs requests for the region you specify here.

Examples:
- `us-east-1` for common Bedrock availability
- `us-west-2` for teams standardizing on West US adjacency

In multi-account or compliance-sensitive setups, choose the region that matches your approved Bedrock access boundary and the models listed in `atlasmind.bedrock.modelIds`.

atlasmind.bedrock.modelIds.description: Amazon Bedrock model IDs AtlasMind should expose as routed models, such as anthropic.claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219-v1:0.
atlasmind.bedrock.modelIds.markdownDescription:
Amazon Bedrock model IDs AtlasMind should expose as routed models. Use the full Bedrock model ID, not a friendly alias.

Examples:
- `["anthropic.claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219-v1:0"]`
- `["anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0", "amazon.nova-pro-v1:0"]`

For larger organizations, use this as an allow-list so AtlasMind only routes to approved Bedrock models.

atlasmind.ssotPath.description: Relative path to the SSOT memory folder. If that path is missing, AtlasMind auto-detects an existing MindAtlas project in the current workspace when possible.
atlasmind.ssotPath.markdownDescription:
Relative path to the SSOT memory folder. AtlasMind expects a safe workspace-relative path and will auto-detect an existing memory folder when possible if the configured path is missing.

Examples:
- Default project memory: `project_memory`
- Monorepo app memory: `apps/api/project_memory`
- Team convention with shared docs folder: `docs/atlasmind-memory`

Prefer stable relative paths so onboarding, imports, and automation all resolve the same folder structure.

atlasmind.showImportProjectAction.description: Show the Import Existing Project toolbar action in the AtlasMind Memory view.
atlasmind.showImportProjectAction.markdownDescription:
Show the Import Existing Project toolbar action in the AtlasMind Memory view. Disable this if you want a cleaner sidebar for already-bootstrapped repositories.

Examples:
- Personal workspace still onboarding projects: `true`
- Locked-down team workspace with a standardized import flow handled elsewhere: `false`

atlasmind.toolApprovalMode.description: How AtlasMind requests approval before running tools with side effects or external execution.
atlasmind.toolApprovalMode.markdownDescription:
How AtlasMind requests approval before running tools with side effects or external execution. This is the main safety dial for automation.

Modes:
- `always-ask`: prompt for every tool action
- `ask-on-write`: allow reads but prompt before writes
- `ask-on-external`: prompt when leaving the editor boundary, such as terminal or network activity
- `allow-safe-readonly`: allow only safe read operations without prompts

Examples:
- Highly regulated repo: `always-ask`
- Normal coding workflow: `ask-on-write`
- Trusted internal automation with careful review: `ask-on-external`
- Read-only investigation or audits: `allow-safe-readonly`

atlasmind.allowTerminalWrite.description: Allow write-capable terminal subprocesses such as installs and commits after explicit approval.
atlasmind.allowTerminalWrite.markdownDescription:
Allow write-capable terminal subprocesses such as installs and commits after explicit approval. This controls whether AtlasMind may use terminal-based commands that can mutate the workspace or repository.

Examples:
- Investigation-only mode: `false`
- Developer sandbox where installs and generators are expected: `true`

Keep this off in repositories where package installation, generated files, or git history changes must go through separate review controls.

atlasmind.autoVerifyAfterWrite.description: Run configured verification scripts after successful workspace write tools such as file-edit, file-write, and git-apply-patch.
atlasmind.autoVerifyAfterWrite.markdownDescription:
Run configured verification scripts after successful workspace write tools such as file-edit, file-write, and git-apply-patch. This helps catch regressions immediately after AtlasMind changes files.

Examples:
- Tight local loop: `true` with `autoVerifyScripts` set to `["test"]`
- Large monorepo where verification is handled elsewhere: `false`
- CI-like local enforcement: `true` with multiple scripts such as `test`, `lint`, and `typecheck`

atlasmind.autoVerifyScripts.description: Package scripts to run after successful write operations. Script names are sanitized and invoked without shell interpolation.
atlasmind.autoVerifyScripts.markdownDescription:
Package scripts to run after successful write operations. AtlasMind runs these as package scripts, not arbitrary shell fragments.

Examples:
- Minimal verification: `["test"]`
- Typical TypeScript project: `["lint", "test", "compile"]`
- Scaled repo with dedicated validation scripts: `["test:unit", "test:manifest", "typecheck"]`

Use short, deterministic scripts that give fast signal after edits.

atlasmind.autoVerifyTimeoutMs.description: Maximum time in milliseconds allowed for each automatic verification script.
atlasmind.autoVerifyTimeoutMs.markdownDescription:
Maximum time in milliseconds allowed for each automatic verification script. This timeout applies per script.

Examples:
- Fast local unit checks: `30000`
- Mixed test and lint pipeline: `120000`
- Large workspace with slower build validation: `300000`

Raise this only when your verification scripts are intentionally slower and still provide useful post-edit feedback.

atlasmind.chatSessionTurnLimit.description: How many recent chat turns AtlasMind carries forward into freeform requests.
atlasmind.chatSessionTurnLimit.markdownDescription:
How many recent chat turns AtlasMind carries forward into freeform requests. Higher values preserve more conversational continuity but increase prompt size.

Examples:
- Short, task-focused chats: `4`
- Default general use: `6`
- Long debugging sessions where prior decisions matter: `10` or higher

Increase this carefully in large team sessions so context remains relevant rather than noisy.

atlasmind.chatSessionContextChars.description: Maximum number of characters reserved for compacted session carry-forward context.
atlasmind.chatSessionContextChars.markdownDescription:
Maximum number of characters reserved for compacted session carry-forward context. This acts as the budget for summarized prior conversation.

Examples:
- Lightweight carry-forward: `1200`
- Default balance: `2500`
- Complex multi-step work with many assumptions to preserve: `4000` to `6000`

Use a higher value only if older conversation state is materially affecting correctness.

atlasmind.projectApprovalFileThreshold.description: Estimated changed-file threshold that triggers /project approval gating.
atlasmind.projectApprovalFileThreshold.markdownDescription:
Estimated changed-file threshold that triggers `/project` approval gating. AtlasMind uses this with other planner heuristics to decide when work is large enough to require explicit confirmation.

Examples:
- Small utility repo: `6`
- Default general-use threshold: `12`
- Monorepo where broader edits are normal: `20` or `30`

Lower this in high-risk repositories where even moderate file churn should pause for review.

atlasmind.projectEstimatedFilesPerSubtas.description: Heuristic multiplier used to estimate changed files from the planned subtask count.
atlasmind.projectEstimatedFilesPerSubtas.markdownDescription:
Heuristic multiplier used to estimate changed files from the planned subtask count. Higher values make AtlasMind assume each subtask fans out across more files.

Examples:
- Tightly scoped codebase with isolated modules: `1`
- Typical service or extension repo: `2`
- Shared-platform or layered monorepo: `3` or `4`

Tune this to match how often a single planned step touches tests, docs, configs, and implementation together.

atlasmind.projectChangedFileReferenceLim.description: Maximum number of changed files surfaced as clickable references after /project runs.
atlasmind.projectChangedFileReferenceLim.markdownDescription:
Maximum number of changed files surfaced as clickable references after `/project` runs. This affects summary readability, not the actual set of changed files.

Examples:
- Compact summaries: `3`
- Default balance: `5`
- Review-heavy workflows where users want more direct navigation: `10`

Keep this lower if `/project` runs often touch many files and you want summaries to stay concise.

atlasmind.projectRunReportFolder.description: Relative folder for persisted /project run summary JSON reports.
atlasmind.projectRunReportFolder.markdownDescription:
Relative folder for persisted `/project` run summary JSON reports. AtlasMind stores operational summaries here for later review or tooling.

Examples:
- Default operational history: `project_memory/operations`
- Separate audit folder inside docs: `docs/atlasmind/runs`
- Centralized automation folder in a monorepo: `ops/atlasmind/run-reports`

Choose a stable relative path that fits your repo's review and retention practices.

atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringEna.description: Scaffold dependency monitoring defaults when AtlasMind bootstraps governance files for a project.
atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringEna.markdownDescription:
Scaffold dependency monitoring defaults when AtlasMind bootstraps governance files for a project. This controls whether bootstrap adds dependency-update automation and SSOT policy starter docs for Atlas-built repositories.

Examples:
- Team template repo where AtlasMind should always wire dependency review automation: `true`
- Minimal experimental repo where governance files stay manual: `false`

Disable this if your organization already provisions dependency automation elsewhere and you do not want AtlasMind creating update-policy files.

atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringPro.description: Dependency monitoring providers AtlasMind should scaffold for project governance baselines.
atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringPro.markdownDescription:
Dependency monitoring providers AtlasMind should scaffold for project governance baselines. AtlasMind currently supports Dependabot, Renovate, Snyk, and Azure DevOps-oriented pipeline scaffolding because those patterns are common in professional repositories and can be represented safely as checked-in config.

Examples:
- GitHub-native default: `["dependabot"]`
- Organizations standardized on Renovate: `["renovate"]`
- Security-driven workflow with Snyk plus Dependabot: `["dependabot", "snyk"]`
- Mixed enterprise rollout: `["dependabot", "renovate", "azure-devops"]`

If this list is empty while dependency monitoring is enabled, AtlasMind still scaffolds SSOT policy docs so teams can document a manual or externally managed review workflow.

atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringSch.description: Default cadence AtlasMind uses when scaffolding dependency monitoring automation.
atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringSch.markdownDescription:
Default cadence AtlasMind uses when scaffolding dependency monitoring automation. AtlasMind maps this into the generated Dependabot or Renovate configuration for Atlas-built projects.

Examples:
- Fast-moving security-sensitive services: `daily`
- Normal team workflow with predictable review windows: `weekly`
- Stable products that prefer fewer update PRs: `monthly`

Choose the slowest cadence that still lets your team review important updates before they become risky backlog.

atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringIss.description: Add a dependency review issue template when AtlasMind scaffolds project governance files.
atlasmind.projectDependencyMonitoringIss.markdownDescription:
Add a dependency review issue template when AtlasMind scaffolds project governance files. The template helps teams record approval decisions, exceptions, and follow-up work after automation detects dependency drift.

Examples:
- Review-heavy engineering org that tracks every deferred or major update: `true`
- Lightweight personal repo with no formal review tickets: `false`

Keep this on when updates must be reviewed by multiple people or linked to compliance evidence.

atlasmind.toolWebhookEnabled.description: Enable outbound webhook delivery for Atlas tool execution events.
atlasmind.toolWebhookEnabled.markdownDescription:
Enable outbound webhook delivery for Atlas tool execution events. When enabled, AtlasMind sends selected tool lifecycle events to the configured webhook endpoint.

Examples:
- Local-only development with no external observers: `false`
- Team telemetry or audit pipeline: `true`

Only enable this when you have an approved HTTPS endpoint and clear expectations about what execution metadata may leave the editor.

atlasmind.toolWebhookUrl.description: HTTPS endpoint that receives Atlas tool execution webhook payloads.
atlasmind.toolWebhookUrl.markdownDescription:
HTTPS endpoint that receives Atlas tool execution webhook payloads. Leave empty unless `atlasmind.toolWebhookEnabled` is turned on.

Examples:
- Internal event collector: `https://ops.example.com/atlasmind/webhooks`
- Local integration test tunnel: `https://1234abcd.ngrok.app/hooks/atlasmind`

Prefer a dedicated ingestion endpoint for scaled deployments so AtlasMind events stay separated from unrelated webhooks.

atlasmind.toolWebhookTimeoutMs.markdownDescription:
Webhook request timeout in milliseconds. This limits how long AtlasMind waits for the webhook receiver before treating delivery as timed out.

Examples:
- Low-latency internal service: `3000`
- Default general use: `5000`
- Cross-region or heavily filtered ingress path: `10000`

Do not raise this unnecessarily, or slow webhook infrastructure can start delaying the editor experience.

atlasmind.toolWebhookEvents.description: Selected tool webhook event names to emit.
atlasmind.toolWebhookEvents.markdownDescription:
Selected tool webhook event names to emit. Choose the smallest set that satisfies your observability or audit needs.

Available events:
- `tool.started`
- `tool.completed`
- `tool.failed`
- `tool.test`

Examples:
- Minimal audit trail: `["tool.failed"]`
- Basic lifecycle monitoring: `["tool.started", "tool.completed", "tool.failed"]`
- Integration testing of receivers: include `tool.test` temporarily

atlasmind.experimentalSkillLearningEnabl.description: Enable Atlas-generated custom skill drafts. Warning: this sends additional model requests and generated code still requires manual safety review.
atlasmind.experimentalSkillLearningEnabl.markdownDescription:
Enable Atlas-generated custom skill drafts. This feature increases model usage and the generated artifacts still require manual review before they are trusted or committed.

Examples:
- Production or regulated workspace: `false`
- Sandbox for experimenting with new skills: `true`

Treat this as an assisted authoring feature, not an autonomous trust boundary.

atlasmind.dailyCostLimitUsd.description: Daily cost limit in USD. Set to 0 to disable. AtlasMind will warn you when 80% and 100% of this limit is reached.
atlasmind.dailyCostLimitUsd.markdownDescription:
Daily cost limit in USD. Set this to `0` to disable the cap. AtlasMind warns at 80% usage and again at 100%.

Examples:
- Unlimited personal experimentation: `0`
- Individual guardrail for a paid account: `5` or `10`
- Shared team budget with tighter daily control per developer: `20` or another agreed cap

Use this with `budgetMode` to combine soft routing preferences and a hard spend boundary.

atlasmind.voice.ttsEnabled.description: Automatically speak @atlas freeform responses aloud using the Voice Panel TTS engine.
atlasmind.voice.ttsEnabled.markdownDescription:
Automatically speak `@atlas` freeform responses aloud using the Voice Panel TTS engine. This affects spoken playback, not text generation.

Examples:
- Hands-free coding or accessibility workflow: `true`
- Quiet office or text-only use: `false`

atlasmind.voice.sttEnabled.description: Show a voice input button in the Voice Panel (STT requires microphone access).
atlasmind.voice.sttEnabled.markdownDescription:
Show a voice input button in the Voice Panel. Speech-to-text requires microphone access and browser or platform support.

Examples:
- Voice-first workflow: `true`
- Locked-down desktop without microphone permissions: `false`

atlasmind.voice.inputDeviceId.description: Preferred microphone device id for supported voice backends. Leave empty to use the system default input device.
atlasmind.voice.inputDeviceId.markdownDescription:
Preferred microphone device id for supported voice backends. Leave it empty to use the browser or OS default input device.

Examples:
- Pin a specific headset mic: a backend-provided device id
- Use the default system microphone: empty string

atlasmind.voice.outputDeviceId.description: Preferred speaker device id for supported voice backends. Leave empty to use the system default output device.
atlasmind.voice.outputDeviceId.markdownDescription:
Preferred speaker device id for supported voice backends. Leave it empty to use the browser or OS default output device.

Examples:
- Route playback to a specific headset or speaker: a backend-provided device id
- Use the default system output device: empty string

atlasmind.voice.rate.description: Speech synthesis rate. 1.0 is normal speed; 0.5 is half speed; 2.0 is double speed.
atlasmind.voice.rate.markdownDescription:
Speech synthesis rate. `1.0` is normal speed, `0.5` is half speed, and `2.0` is double speed.

Examples:
- Accessibility or careful listening: `0.8`
- Default conversational playback: `1.0`
- Fast review of long responses: `1.25` or `1.5`

atlasmind.voice.pitch.description: Speech synthesis pitch. 1.0 is the default pitch.
atlasmind.voice.pitch.markdownDescription:
Speech synthesis pitch. `1.0` is the default pitch for the selected voice.

Examples:
- Lower, flatter speech profile: `0.8`
- Default voice output: `1.0`
- Brighter or more animated playback: `1.2`

Use this mainly for comfort and intelligibility rather than correctness.

atlasmind.voice.volume.description: Speech synthesis volume (0 = silent, 1 = full volume).
atlasmind.voice.volume.markdownDescription:
Speech synthesis volume where `0` is silent and `1` is full volume.

Examples:
- Shared office or quiet environment: `0.3` to `0.5`
- Normal headset use: `0.8` to `1.0`
- Muted while keeping TTS enabled for quick toggling: `0`

atlasmind.voice.language.description: BCP 47 language tag for voice synthesis and recognition (e.g. 'en-US', 'fr-FR'). Leave empty to use the browser/OS default.
atlasmind.voice.language.markdownDescription:
BCP 47 language tag for voice synthesis and recognition, such as `en-US` or `fr-FR`. Leave empty to use the browser or OS default.

Examples:
- US English: `en-US`
- British English: `en-GB`
- French (France): `fr-FR`
- Default system language: empty string

Set this explicitly in shared environments where predictable voice language matters.

atlasmind.maxToolIterations.description: Maximum tool-call loop iterations per agent turn.
atlasmind.maxToolIterations.markdownDescription:
Maximum tool-call loop iterations per agent turn. This limits how many sequential rounds of tool use the agent can perform before it must stop and answer.

Examples:
- Conservative environment: `5`
- Default balance: `10`
- Complex workflows that genuinely need longer tool chains: `15` to `20`

Raise this carefully, because higher values can increase latency and unintended automation depth.

atlasmind.maxToolCallsPerTurn.description: Maximum parallel tool calls the model may issue in a single turn.
atlasmind.maxToolCallsPerTurn.markdownDescription:
Maximum parallel tool calls the model may issue in a single turn. This caps fan-out when the model wants to gather or verify data quickly.

Examples:
- Minimal concurrency for predictable behavior: `3`
- Default general use: `8`
- Large codebase exploration with many independent reads: `12` or `16`

Higher values can improve speed for read-heavy work but may produce noisier or more expensive turns.

atlasmind.toolExecutionTimeoutMs.markdownDescription:
Per-tool execution timeout in milliseconds. If a tool call exceeds this limit, AtlasMind treats it as timed out.

Examples:
- Very fast local commands only: `5000`
- Default mixed workload: `15000`
- Slower file-system or network-backed tools: `30000`

Tune this alongside tool approval and webhook settings so slow integrations do not degrade the interactive experience.

atlasmind.providerTimeoutMs.description: Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for a model provider response.
atlasmind.providerTimeoutMs.markdownDescription:
Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for a model provider response. This covers the provider round-trip, not local tool execution.

Examples:
- Fast local or LAN-hosted models: `15000`
- Default cloud-provider balance: `30000`
- Slower premium reasoning models or distant regions: `45000` to `60000`

Use a higher timeout when you intentionally allow slower reasoning models through `speedMode` or provider-specific routing.
